title: Auth-Gated API
description: Identity-first serverless backend. Cognito handles user registration and authentication, issuing JWTs that API Gateway validates before forwarding requests to Lambda. Lambda reads secrets at runtime from Secrets Manager and writes state to DynamoDB. No servers, no VPC, and auth is enforced at the API layer — not in application code.
practices: Validate JWTs at API Gateway using a Cognito authorizer — never re-validate tokens in Lambda. API Gateway rejects unauthenticated requests before Lambda is even invoked.
Use Lambda environment variables to store the Secrets Manager secret ARN, not the secret value. Fetch the value at runtime and cache it for the function lifetime.
Cognito User Pool Clients should use server-side auth flows (USER_SRP_AUTH) for backends, not client-side flows that expose credentials.
Lambda IAM roles should grant DynamoDB access on specific table ARNs only — never use wildcard (*) resource in production.
Enable MFA at least as OPTIONAL on the Cognito pool. Require it for admin operations.
SecretsManager secret rotation should be enabled for any credentials that support it (RDS, Redshift, DocumentDB).
configNotes: API Gateway stage_name (e.g., prod) is the deployment stage identifier. For HTTP/WebSocket APIs it becomes the aws_apigatewayv2_stage name; for REST APIs it creates a separate aws_api_gateway_stage resource.
Lambda requires runtime, handler, memory, and timeout.
DynamoDB billing_mode PAY_PER_REQUEST is preferred for variable traffic serverless patterns.
SecretsManager secret_name should use a path convention like prod/myapp/api-key.
